skill.name: ai-image-generation
skill.description: Generate, edit, upscale, and stitch AI images through the inference.sh CLI using FLUX, Gemini, Grok, Seedream, Reve, and related image apps.
skill.summary: Create AI images and image edits with inference.sh models from Claude, Codex, or Claude Code.
skill.author: toolshell

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer flagged many Ruby or shell backtick patterns, but the evidence is Markdown inline code and bash examples, not Ruby execution. The skill does intentionally allow Bash access to the inference.sh CLI and sends prompts or image URLs to remote image apps, so the final risk is medium. No evidence found for prompt injection, credential exfiltration, weak cryptography, or unauthorized filesystem access.

medium_findings.title: Networked External Command Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill grants Bash access to infsh commands and gives examples for login, app listing, and remote image generation. This matches the skill purpose, but prompts and image URLs can be sent to external inference.sh apps.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed tool and examples directly show execution of the infsh CLI against remote apps. No arbitrary shell construction or hidden malicious endpoint was found, so this is an elevated operational risk.

medium_findings.title: Remote Skill Installation Guidance
medium_findings.description: The documentation instructs users to run npx skills add commands for related remote skills. This can install additional third-party skill content outside the reviewed file, so users should review those packages separately.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The related-skill commands are explicit and legitimate documentation, but they can pull unreviewed external content. The risk depends on the referenced packages at install time.

low_findings.title: Dismissed Markdown Backtick Execution Alerts
low_findings.description: The static Ruby or shell backtick alerts point to Markdown code fences, inline command examples, model names, and app IDs. These lines are documentation text, not executable Ruby backticks.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are Markdown tables or fenced bash examples in SKILL.md. There is no Ruby source file, interpolation path, or execution wrapper in the reviewed file.

low_findings.title: Dismissed Weak Cryptography Alert
low_findings.description: The static weak cryptography alert points to the YAML description line. That line contains marketing text and model names, with no cryptographic algorithm, key handling, hashing, or encryption logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is a natural-language description. No code or cryptographic operation appears at that location.

low_findings.title: Benign Hardcoded External URLs
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are service documentation links, a display image, and placeholder image URL examples. They are expected for this skill, but they indicate reliance on external web resources.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL locations are visible documentation, image, or placeholder examples. No evidence found that they exfiltrate secrets or contact an unrelated endpoint.

dangerous_patterns.title: Allowed Bash Command Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill allows Bash execution for commands matching infsh. This is necessary for the workflow but should be treated as an external command and network boundary.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration directly grants Bash access to infsh commands. The pattern is explicit and limited to the CLI named in the skill.

content.value_statement: Teams need a consistent way to create AI images without memorizing every model command. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through inference.sh workflows for generation, editing, upscaling, and stitching.
actual_capabilities: Runs inference.sh image apps through the infsh CLI.
Generates images from text prompts with FLUX, Gemini, Grok, Seedream, and Reve examples.
Shows model choices for text rendering, cinematic output, fast generation, and upscaling.
Supports image-to-image style workflows through image URL inputs where the selected app supports them.
Lists related inference.sh skills for upscaling, background removal, video, and avatar workflows.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh CLI to be installed and authenticated.
Image quality, speed, price, and availability depend on the selected remote model.
Prompts and image URLs may be processed by external inference.sh apps.
The reviewed skill is documentation only and does not include local model code.

use_cases.title: Create Campaign Visuals
use_cases.description: Generate draft social media graphics, ad concepts, and marketing images from written creative briefs.

use_cases.title: Prototype Product Imagery
use_cases.description: Create product mockups and visual options before scheduling photography or design production.

use_cases.title: Prepare Visual Assets
use_cases.description: Upscale, stitch, or edit image assets for presentations, documentation, and creative reviews.

prompt_templates.title: Generate a Simple Image
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate an image of a modern desk setup with natural light. Use a reliable inference.sh image model and explain the selected model.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Model Choices
prompt_templates.scenario: Choose a model for a specific style
prompt_templates.prompt: I need a cinematic 16:9 landscape for a product launch slide. Compare two suitable inference.sh image models, then generate one version.

prompt_templates.title: Create Text in an Image
prompt_templates.scenario: Use a model suited for text rendering
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a poster image that clearly displays the words NEW RELEASE. Pick an inference.sh model that handles text rendering well.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Multi-Step Image Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate three product concept images, select the strongest option, then upscale it. Keep the prompt and model choices documented.

output_examples.input: A user asks for a studio product image of a ceramic coffee mug.
output_examples.output: The assistant selects a suitable image model, generates a polished product mockup, and returns the image result with the prompt used.

output_examples.input: A designer needs a wide cinematic background for a landing page hero.
output_examples.output: The assistant chooses a model that supports wide aspect ratios and creates a landscape image suitable for layout testing.

output_examples.input: A creator has an image URL that needs higher resolution.
output_examples.output: The assistant uses an upscaling image app and returns an enhanced version while noting that the source URL was sent to the remote service.

best_practices: Review prompts and image URLs before sending confidential material to remote apps.
Choose models based on the needed output, such as text rendering, speed, 4K quality, or upscaling.
Record the prompt, selected app, and key settings so successful results can be repeated.
anti_patterns: Do not send private customer images or unreleased product photos without approval.
Do not assume every listed model supports the same inputs or aspect ratios.
Do not install related skills from remote sources without reviewing their contents separately.

faq.question: Does this skill generate images locally?
faq.answer: No. It uses the inference.sh CLI to call remote image generation apps.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What setup is required?
faq.answer: You need the infsh CLI installed and an authenticated inference.sh account.

faq.question: Can it upscale images?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes an example for the Topaz image upscaler app.

faq.question: Can it render text inside images?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill points to Reve and Seedream examples for text rendering use cases.

faq.question: Is it safe to use with sensitive images?
faq.answer: Use caution. Prompts and image URLs may be sent to external inference.sh apps.
